past_medical_history: Up-to-date on vaccinations including meningococcal and HPV series
family_history: Mother: Hashimoto's thyroiditis, managed with levothyroxine
Sister age 22: celiac disease diagnosed at age 16
No family history of epilepsy, psychotic disorders, or neurological disease

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinking at college parties, 2-3 drinks per occasion, about twice monthly
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any drug use per roommate. Roommate states patient has never used marijuana, psychedelics, or any other substances.
social_history.living_situation: Lives in college dormitory with a roommate. Active in campus intramural basketball.
social_history.recent_stressors: First semester of college; adjusting to pre-med coursework. No major psychosocial stressors per roommate.

neurological_exam.mental_status: GCS 12 (E3V3M6). Agitated and disoriented. Unable to provide coherent history. Speech is disorganized with frequent neologisms and word salad. Intermittently responds to internal stimuli — appears to be experiencing visual hallucinations (talking to invisible people, appearing terrified of something in the corner). Unable to cooperate with formal cognitive testing. Not oriented to person, place, or time.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal 4 mm, reactive to light bilaterally. Oculocephalic movements intact. Prominent orofacial dyskinesias — repetitive lip smacking, jaw movements, and tongue protrusion occurring every 10-20 seconds. Facial sensation intact to grimace. No facial asymmetry. Hearing appears grossly intact (startles to loud sounds). Gag reflex intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Unable to cooperate with formal strength testing. Grossly moves all four extremities spontaneously. Intermittent dystonic posturing of the left arm — sustained wrist flexion and finger extension lasting 5-10 seconds. Bilateral upper extremity catatonic posturing observed when patient is stimulated — arms held in fixed elevated position (waxy flexibility). Tone increased in bilateral upper extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Unable to formally assess due to agitation and inability to cooperate.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 3+ globally — bilateral biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles hyperreflexia. Bilateral extensor plantar responses (Babinski positive bilaterally).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess due to agitation.
neurological_exam.gait: Not assessed — patient unable to stand safely.
neurological_exam.additional: During the 45-minute examination, three episodes of intermittent staring spells observed lasting 20-40 seconds each — during these episodes, the patient becomes motionless with fixed gaze, orofacial automatisms (lip smacking), and left arm tonic posturing. Afterwards, he is briefly more confused before returning to his agitated baseline. These episodes are highly suspicious for seizures. Autonomic instability present: blood pressure fluctuating between 110/68 and 158/94, heart rate ranging from 88 to 128, and diaphoresis. Temperature 38.2°C without clear infectious source.

patient.chief_complaint: Acute psychosis — brought by campus police
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Sean O'Brien is a 19-year-old right-handed Irish-American college freshman (pre-medical student) who was brought to the emergency department by campus police after being found in his dorm room talking to invisible people, smashing objects, and appearing terrified. His roommate, who has known him for 4 months, provides the history as the patient is unable to give a coherent account. The roommate reports that approximately 10 days ago, Sean began acting 'off' — he was more withdrawn than usual, skipped several classes (very uncharacteristic for an academically motivated student), stopped going to basketball practice, and was not eating well. He also complained of a headache and felt 'warm' but did not take his temperature. The roommate initially attributed this to first-semester stress or a mild illness. About 5 days ago, Sean became noticeably paranoid — he accused his roommate of poisoning his water bottle and began locking his belongings in his desk drawer. He started sleeping erratically, sometimes not sleeping at all and pacing the room at night. Three days ago, the roommate witnessed Sean talking to people who were not there, appearing to see things in the corner of the room, and becoming increasingly agitated and combative. He smashed his laptop and threw books across the room. The roommate called campus police this morning when Sean was found standing rigidly in the middle of the room, unresponsive to voice, with his arms held in a fixed position above his head for several minutes before suddenly screaming and attempting to climb out the window. Campus police brought him to the ED in restraints. The roommate emphatically denies any drug use by the patient — they share a small room and the roommate states he would know if Sean used any substances. Sean has no history of psychiatric illness, no prior behavioral episodes, and was functioning at a high level academically until 10 days ago. No family history of schizophrenia or bipolar disorder. The roommate notes that Sean's mother has thyroid problems and his older sister has 'some food allergy disease.' There is no history of head trauma.

findings.frequency: 1-3 Hz delta waves with superimposed 20-30 Hz beta activity riding on the delta waves
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta activity with overlying fast (beta) bursts creating a 'brush-like' appearance on each delta wave. Pattern is continuous and persistent.
findings.state: Present in wakefulness and persisting into drowsiness/sleep

findings.location: Right temporal onset (T4/T6) with secondary generalization
findings.frequency: Rhythmic 5 Hz theta evolving to 2 Hz spike-wave over 65 seconds
findings.morphology: Evolving rhythmic activity with increasing amplitude and decreasing frequency
findings.state: During apparent behavioral arrest with orofacial automatisms

findings.location: Right temporal onset with spread to bilateral temporal regions
findings.frequency: Similar morphology to seizure #1, duration 48 seconds

artifacts.description: Frequent movement and muscle artifact during periods of agitation, partially limiting interpretation of some segments

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG. Two electrographic seizures of right temporal origin were captured during this 2-hour recording, both correlating with clinical episodes of behavioral arrest and orofacial automatisms. Background is moderately to severely disorganized with diffuse theta-delta slowing. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Intermittent movement artifact during agitation; some segments partially obscured.

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes (hippocampi and amygdalae), right greater than left
findings.size: Diffuse signal abnormality, not a discrete mass

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, subtle but definite, right > left

findings.borders: Ill-defined, blending into adjacent mesial temporal structures

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
mri.impression: Subtle bilateral medial temporal lobe FLAIR hyperintensity, right greater than left, without enhancement or restricted diffusion. The bilateral but asymmetric distribution is nonspecific. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 112 bpm, likely from agitation and autonomic instability. No conduction abnormalities.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 11.8 x10^9/L (H), Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Chloride 96 mEq/L (L), CRP 12 mg/L (H), ESR 28 mm/hr (H), CK 380 U/L (H).

special_tests.Oligoclonal_bands: 4 bands present in CSF, not present in matched serum (CSF-specific)
special_tests.Anti_NMDA_receptor_antibodies: Sent — results pending (send-out test, estimated 7-10 days)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cmH2O. WBC: 45 cells/uL (elevated) (3% PMN/92% lymph). Protein: 55 mg/dL. Glucose: 58 mg/dL (ratio 0.61 (serum glucose 95 mg/dL)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen. Bacterial_culture: No growth at 48 hours. HSV_1_PCR: Negative. HSV_2_PCR: Negative. VZV_PCR: Negative. Enterovirus_PCR: Negative. Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells. Oligoclonal_bands: 4 bands present in CSF, not present in matched serum (CSF-specific).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:128)  (H), Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:64)  (H).

Testicular Ultrasound.value: Heterogeneous 1.4 cm mass in the lower pole of the right testis with mixed echogenicity — contains solid and cystic components with a small echogenic focus suggesting calcification or fat. No increased vascularity on Doppler.
Normal size (4.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm), homogeneous echotexture, normal Doppler flow

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Right testis Heterogeneous 1.4 cm mass in the lower pole of the right testis with mixed echogenicity — contains solid and cystic components with a small echogenic focus suggesting calcification or fat. No increased vascularity on Doppler.  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Right testicular mass 1.4 cm, consistent with teratoma

signal_characteristics.CT: Mixed density mass with fat attenuation (-30 HU) and calcification

output.impression: No evidence of metastatic disease — no retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, no pulmonary metastases, no other masses. Clinical stage I testicular teratoma. Clinical correlation recommended.

Psychiatric Consultation Note.value: Atypical presentation for primary psychotic disorder. While the acute psychosis with paranoia, hallucinations, and disorganized behavior could suggest first-episode schizophrenia or brief psychotic disorder, several features are highly atypical: (1) hyperacute onset over days rather than the typical insidious prodrome of schizophrenia, (2) prominent orofacial dyskinesias and dystonic posturing — true movement disorder, not medication-induced, (3) catatonic features with waxy flexibility, (4) autonomic instability (blood pressure and heart rate fluctuations, fever), (5) seizures confirmed on EEG, (6) negative drug screen, (7) age of 19 in a male is earlier than typical schizophrenia onset peak (early-to-mid 20s). Recommend urgent neurological workup including autoimmune encephalitis panel.

Defer antipsychotic initiation. Use benzodiazepines (lorazepam) for acute agitation management. The movement disorder and seizures argue against primary psychiatric diagnosis. If autoimmune encephalitis is excluded, reconsider psychiatric diagnosis.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Clinical Impression Atypical presentation for primary psychotic disorder. While the acute psychosis with paranoia, hallucinations, and disorganized behavior could suggest first-episode schizophrenia or brief psychotic disorder, several features are highly atypical: (1) hyperacute onset over days rather than the typical insidious prodrome of schizophrenia, (2) prominent orofacial dyskinesias and dystonic posturing — true movement disorder, not medication-induced, (3) catatonic features with waxy flexibility, (4) autonomic instability (blood pressure and heart rate fluctuations, fever), (5) seizures confirmed on EEG, (6) negative drug screen, (7) age of 19 in a male is earlier than typical schizophrenia onset peak (early-to-mid 20s). Recommend urgent neurological workup including autoimmune encephalitis panel.  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Atypical psychotic presentation with red flags for organic etiology

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis associated with testicular teratoma

differential.key_distinguishing: Acute onset with fever, prominent movement disorder (orofacial dyskinesias, dystonia), catatonia, autonomic instability, and seizures are all atypical for primary psychosis. Schizophrenia typically has an insidious prodrome over months and does not cause seizures, dyskinesias, or autonomic instability.

Temporal lobe FLAIR changes can occur in both conditions, but HSV PCR is negative, there is no hemorrhagic change on MRI, and extreme delta brush on EEG is specific for NMDAR encephalitis, not seen in HSV.

Negative comprehensive urine drug screen and negative blood alcohol. The persistent and worsening course over 10 days is inconsistent with drug-induced psychosis, which typically resolves within days of cessation.

differential.diagnosis: Autoimmune encephalitis (other antibodies — LGI1, CASPR2, GABA)
differential.key_distinguishing: Extreme delta brush on EEG plus orofacial dyskinesias plus catatonia is a highly specific clinical triad for NMDAR encephalitis specifically, not other autoimmune encephalitides.

optimal_actions.action: Ensure patient safety: restraints if necessary, IV access, continuous telemetry and pulse oximetry. Use benzodiazepines (lorazepam 2 mg IV) for acute agitation — avoid antipsychotics.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient stabilization without masking neurological symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, CRP, CK, TSH, hepatic panel, urine drug screen, blood alcohol, ammonia, HIV, RPR
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative drug screen rules out substance-induced psychosis; mild hyponatremia and elevated CK

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle bilateral medial temporal FLAIR changes — non-specific but consistent with encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, HSV PCR, oligoclonal bands, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies (CSF, not just serum)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, CSF-specific oligoclonal bands, negative HSV PCR, NMDAR antibodies pending

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG — recognize extreme delta brush as nearly pathognomonic for NMDAR encephalitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Extreme delta brush pattern plus electrographic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h pending HSV PCR results — always cover HSV in undifferentiated encephalitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric HSV coverage until PCR returns negative

optimal_actions.action: Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x 5 days + IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) once NMDAR antibodies confirmed positive
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Immunotherapy to halt antibody-mediated neuronal damage

optimal_actions.action: Order testicular ultrasound to screen for teratoma — in males, testicular teratoma is the most common associated tumor
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right testicular mass consistent with teratoma

optimal_actions.action: Consult urology for radical inguinal orchiectomy — tumor removal improves neurological outcomes
Initiate continuous EEG monitoring for subclinical seizures and start anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 1000 mg IV BID)
If insufficient improvement after first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy (rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 doses)
Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for staging of teratoma and to exclude other tumors
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No metastatic disease; stage I teratoma

critical_actions: Order NMDAR antibodies in CSF (not just serum — CSF is more sensitive)
Start empiric acyclovir pending HSV PCR (always cover HSV in encephalitis)
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone + IVIG or plasmapheresis) once NMDAR confirmed
Screen for teratoma — in males, order testicular ultrasound (ovarian teratoma screening in females)
Orchiectomy for testicular teratoma (tumor removal improves outcomes)
Continuous EEG monitoring for subclinical seizures
contraindicated_actions: Treating with antipsychotics alone without autoimmune workup
Attributing presentation to primary psychiatric illness without ruling out organic causes
Discharging from psychiatric unit without neurological evaluation
Delaying immunotherapy while waiting for antibody results if clinical suspicion is high
key_reasoning_points: NMDAR encephalitis in males is underrecognized — ~20% of cases are male, often without tumor or with testicular teratoma
Extreme delta brush on EEG is nearly pathognomonic for NMDAR encephalitis
Acute psychosis + movement disorder + seizures + autonomic instability = autoimmune encephalitis until proven otherwise
Orofacial dyskinesias (lip smacking, jaw movements) are highly characteristic of NMDAR encephalitis
Family autoimmune history (Hashimoto's, celiac) increases the pre-test probability of autoimmune encephalitis
In young men with NMDAR encephalitis, testicular ultrasound is essential — teratomas are the most common associated tumor
Negative urine drug screen + negative HSV PCR with persistent symptoms should redirect away from toxic/infectious etiologies

red_herrings.intended_effect: NMDAR encephalitis is classically associated with young women — male presentation may not be considered
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: ~20% of NMDAR encephalitis cases occur in males; maintain suspicion regardless of sex

red_herrings.data_point: Campus police involvement suggesting substance use
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Negative drug screen eliminates this; acute behavioral change in a previously well student demands organic workup

red_herrings.intended_effect: May suggest HSV encephalitis as the primary diagnosis
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Both NMDAR encephalitis and HSV can cause temporal signal changes — HSV PCR negative redirects to autoimmune

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: NMDAR encephalitis in a male patient (atypical demographics) presenting with acute psychosis, initially transported by campus police suggesting substance use. The psychiatric-first presentation, male sex, and police involvement all create cognitive biases away from the correct diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis with testicular teratoma.
clinical_pearls: Extreme delta brush on EEG is seen in ~30% of NMDAR encephalitis cases and is virtually never seen in other conditions
NMDAR encephalitis in males accounts for ~20% of cases; testicular teratoma is the most common associated tumor
Normal serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-HCG, LDH) are expected in mature teratoma — do not rely on markers to exclude teratoma
The clinical tetrad of psychosis + movement disorder + seizures + autonomic instability should trigger autoimmune encephalitis workup regardless of demographics
Tumor removal (orchiectomy) improves neurological recovery and reduces relapse risk
